metadata.title: Indian Family Law Statutes — Qdrant VDB Edition
metadata.description: Comprehensive collection of Indian family law acts and sections optimized for semantic search and retrieval. Each entry is a self-contained chunk with embeddings-ready short_summary, rich keywords, and scenario_tags for query matching.
metadata.schema_note: Each chunk has: chunk_id, act_id, act_name, year, applicability, section_number, title, short_summary, detailed_notes, keywords, scenario_tags, related_sections. Use short_summary as the embedding text and all other fields as Qdrant payload.
metadata.source_note: Based on official Indian statutes. Always verify full texts at indiacode.nic.in.

chunks.title: Nature and Evolution of Family under Hindu Law
chunks.short_summary: Hindu family law evolved from ancient texts like Manusmriti and Yajnavalkya Smriti. The joint family system and concept of dharma are foundational. Two main schools: Mitakshara and Dayabhaga govern different aspects.
chunks.detailed_notes: The Hindu family is primarily joint and undivided. Law evolved through Shruti, Smriti, and judicial interpretation. The joint family includes all persons lineally descended from a common ancestor.

chunks.title: Sphere of Personal Law and Persons Governed by Hindu Law
chunks.short_summary: Hindu Law applies to Hindus by religion, including converts, as well as Buddhists, Jains, and Sikhs. Persons domiciled in India not governed by Muslim, Christian, or Parsi law may also be covered. Tribal communities following customary laws may be excluded.
chunks.detailed_notes: Coverage extends to illegitimate children of Hindu fathers, legitimate children of one Hindu parent where raised Hindu. Excludes converts to Islam, Christianity, or Zoroastrianism.

chunks.title: Sources of Hindu Law
chunks.short_summary: Primary sources: Shruti (Vedas), Smriti (Manusmriti, Yajnavalkya Smriti), Customs and usages, Digests and Commentaries. Secondary source: Justice, equity, and good conscience. Judicial decisions and legislation are modern sources.
chunks.detailed_notes: Shruti is considered divine revelation; Smriti is recollection. Custom must be ancient, certain, reasonable, and not opposed to public policy. Digests like Mitakshara and Dayabhaga reconcile conflicting Smritis.

chunks.title: Schools of Hindu Law — Mitakshara and Dayabhaga
chunks.short_summary: Mitakshara school prevails throughout India except Bengal and Assam. Dayabhaga school applies in Bengal and Assam. Key difference: under Mitakshara, coparcenary right by birth; under Dayabhaga, only upon death of ancestor. Affects inheritance and partition.
chunks.detailed_notes: Mitakshara sub-schools: Benaras, Mithila, Maharashtra, Dravida (Madras). Dayabhaga authored by Jimutavahana. In Mitakshara, a son acquires interest in joint property by birth; in Dayabhaga, only on father's death.

chunks.title: Hindu Joint Family and Karta
chunks.short_summary: Hindu joint family consists of common ancestor and all lineal male descendants with wives and unmarried daughters. Karta is the senior-most male member who manages family affairs. Karta has power of alienation for legal necessity, benefit of estate, or pious obligations.
chunks.detailed_notes: Karta's powers include managing property, representing family in legal matters, alienating property with restriction. Alienation without legal necessity can be challenged by coparceners within their lifetime. After 2005 amendment, daughters are also coparceners.

chunks.title: Joint Family Partition and Impartible Property
chunks.short_summary: Partition of Hindu joint family can be by metes and bounds (physical division) or by a definite, unequivocal declaration of intention to separate. Any coparcener can demand partition. Impartible property (e.g., jagirs, samsthans) cannot be divided and devolves by primogeniture.
chunks.detailed_notes: Partial partition is possible (of specific property or between specific members). Partition once made is final unless reopened for fraud. Minor's share protected by court. Reopening permitted if a coparcener was excluded.

chunks.title: Hindu Religious Endowments
chunks.short_summary: Property dedicated to Hindu religious or charitable purposes forms an endowment. Managed by Shebait (for idols) or trustees. The idol is a juristic person. Shebait manages as owner-trustee; income used for religious purposes. Courts oversee mismanagement.
chunks.detailed_notes: Public endowments are subject to state legislation (e.g., HR&CE Acts). Private endowments govern family deities. Shebait's office is hereditary unless otherwise stated. Misappropriation actionable under civil and criminal law.

chunks.title: Short Title, Extent and Commencement
chunks.short_summary: This Act is called the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955. It extends to the whole of India. It came into force from 18 May 1955.
chunks.detailed_notes: Erstwhile Jammu & Kashmir was excluded earlier but now covered after J&K Reorganisation Act 2019.

chunks.title: Application of Act
chunks.short_summary: Applies to any person who is a Hindu by religion, including Virashaiva, Lingayat, followers of Brahmo/Prarthana/Arya Samaj, and persons who are Buddhist, Jain or Sikh by religion. Also applies to persons domiciled in India who are not Muslim, Christian, Parsi, or Jew.
chunks.detailed_notes: Does not apply to Scheduled Tribes unless Central Government notifies. The Act also covers converts to Hinduism.

chunks.title: Definitions
chunks.short_summary: Defines key terms: 'custom' means long-established rule with continuous observance; 'district court' means principal civil court; 'full blood'/'half blood'/'uterine blood' refer to degree of relationship; 'prescribed' means prescribed by rules; 'sapinda relationship' and 'degrees of prohibited relationship' defined with reference to generations.
chunks.detailed_notes: Sapinda relationship: a person is sapinda to another within 3 degrees on mother's side and 5 degrees on father's side. Degrees of prohibited relationship bar marriage between close relatives.

chunks.title: Overriding Effect of Act
chunks.short_summary: The Hindu Marriage Act overrides all previous laws, texts, rules, and customs governing Hindu marriages, to the extent they are inconsistent with this Act.
chunks.detailed_notes: Codification supersedes Smriti rules and local customs unless expressly saved by Section 29.

chunks.title: Conditions for a Valid Hindu Marriage
chunks.short_summary: A Hindu marriage is valid only if: (i) neither party has a living spouse (monogamy); (ii) neither party is of unsound mind, incapable of consent, or suffering from mental disorder; (iii) bridegroom is at least 21 years and bride at least 18 years old; (iv) parties are not within prohibited degrees of relationship unless custom permits; (v) parties are not sapinda of each other unless custom permits.
chunks.detailed_notes: Violation of (i) renders marriage void (Section 11). Violation of (iv) and (v) also void. Violation of (ii) and (iii) makes marriage voidable (Section 12). Child marriage is an offence under Section 18 and PCMA 2006.

chunks.title: Guardianship in Marriage
chunks.short_summary: Where the bride is a minor (below 18), consent of her guardian is required for a valid marriage. The guardian hierarchy is father, mother, paternal grandfather, paternal grandmother, then other relatives.
chunks.detailed_notes: This section is largely redundant now given the minimum age of 18 for brides. PCMA 2006 further restricts child marriages.

chunks.title: Ceremonies for a Hindu Marriage
chunks.short_summary: A Hindu marriage may be solemnized according to the customary rites and ceremonies of either party. Where Saptapadi is part of ceremony (seven steps around sacred fire), the marriage is complete and binding upon the seventh step.
chunks.detailed_notes: Saptapadi is the most common ceremony and legally recognized. Other ceremonies like Gandharva or Asura forms recognized by custom. Mere registration without ceremony does not constitute marriage.

chunks.title: Registration of Hindu Marriages
chunks.short_summary: State Governments may make rules for registration of Hindu marriages in a Hindu Marriage Register. Registration is not mandatory for validity of marriage but provides evidentiary value. Parties may register marriage after solemnization.
chunks.detailed_notes: Supreme Court in Seema v. Ashwani Kumar (2006) directed all states to make marriage registration compulsory. Registration does not cure an invalid marriage.

chunks.title: Restitution of Conjugal Rights
chunks.short_summary: When either spouse has, without reasonable excuse, withdrawn from the society of the other, the aggrieved spouse may apply to the district court for restitution of conjugal rights. The court may issue a decree for restitution if satisfied that the withdrawal was without reasonable cause.
chunks.detailed_notes: Burden of proof of reasonable excuse lies on the respondent. Non-compliance with decree can be enforced by attachment of property. Constitutionality challenged but upheld by Supreme Court. Cannot be used to coerce a spouse to return.

chunks.title: Judicial Separation
chunks.short_summary: Either party to a marriage may present a petition for judicial separation on any of the grounds listed in Section 13 (divorce grounds). Upon judicial separation, the parties are relieved of the duty to cohabit but the marriage is not dissolved.
chunks.detailed_notes: After one year of judicial separation, either party can seek divorce on grounds of non-resumption of cohabitation. It is an intermediate relief short of divorce.

chunks.title: Void Marriages
chunks.short_summary: A Hindu marriage is void if it contravenes Section 5 clause (i) [bigamy/polygamy], clause (iv) [prohibited degrees of relationship], or clause (v) [sapinda relationship]. Such a marriage is null and void from the beginning.
chunks.detailed_notes: A void marriage has no legal effect — no rights of inheritance, no legitimacy of children unless saved by Section 16. A decree of nullity can still be obtained for clarity.

chunks.title: Voidable Marriages
chunks.short_summary: A Hindu marriage is voidable if: (a) the respondent was impotent at time of marriage; (b) either party was of unsound mind, unfit for procreation or suffering from recurrent mental disorder; (c) consent was obtained by force or fraud; (d) the respondent was pregnant by someone other than the petitioner at time of marriage.
chunks.detailed_notes: Unlike void marriages, voidable marriages are valid until annulled by court decree. Petition must be filed within one year of discovering fraud/force. Petitioner must not have lived with respondent after discovering the ground.

chunks.title: Divorce
chunks.short_summary: Either spouse may petition for divorce on grounds of: adultery; cruelty (physical or mental); desertion for 2 years; conversion to non-Hindu religion; incurable mental disorder; venereal disease in communicable form; leprosy; renunciation of the world (sanyas); presumption of death (missing for 7 years). Wife additionally: husband's prior marriage, rape/sodomy/bestiality, non-resumption of cohabitation after maintenance order, marriage solemnized before 15 years of age.
chunks.detailed_notes: Cruelty includes mental cruelty (NRI abandonment, dowry harassment). Desertion requires animus deserendi plus factual separation. Conversion automatically dissolves nothing — petition is needed. After 2005, irretrievable breakdown proposed but not yet formally enacted except through judicial interpretation.

chunks.title: Alternate Relief in Divorce Proceedings
chunks.short_summary: In any proceeding for divorce under Section 13, the court may instead grant a decree for judicial separation if it considers that to be just and proper in the circumstances, and the divorce sought is not justified.
chunks.detailed_notes: Gives court discretion to grant lesser relief (judicial separation) instead of divorce, protecting interests of the family especially children.

chunks.title: Divorce by Mutual Consent
chunks.short_summary: Both spouses may jointly petition for divorce by mutual consent after living separately for one year or more. A second motion must be moved after six months (cooling-off period) and within 18 months of first motion. Court satisfies itself of free consent before granting decree.
chunks.detailed_notes: Supreme Court in Amardeep Singh (2017) held six-month cooling-off period can be waived in exceptional circumstances. Both parties must consent at the time of second motion too.

chunks.title: Divorce on Ground of Irretrievable Breakdown (Proposed)
chunks.short_summary: Though not yet enacted as a standalone provision by Parliament, courts have increasingly used irretrievable breakdown of marriage as a ground for divorce under Article 142 powers of the Supreme Court. The Marriage Laws (Amendment) Bill proposed this as a formal ground but was not passed.
chunks.detailed_notes: Supreme Court in Naveen Kohli v. Neelu Kohli (2006) recommended legislative recognition. Courts use it sparingly under special circumstances exercising Article 142 jurisdiction.

chunks.title: Wife's Right to Oppose Divorce on Irretrievable Breakdown
chunks.short_summary: In a proposed irretrievable breakdown framework, the wife has the right to oppose the grant of divorce if it would cause her grave financial hardship, and the court considers it just to refuse the decree having regard to all circumstances.
chunks.detailed_notes: Protects vulnerable wives from being divorced solely on husband's petition when breakdown is attributed to husband's fault and wife would suffer financial ruin.

chunks.title: No Petition for Divorce in First Year of Marriage
chunks.short_summary: No petition for divorce may be presented within one year of marriage except in cases of exceptional hardship to the petitioner or exceptional depravity of the respondent. Leave of court is required.
chunks.detailed_notes: Court considers child's welfare and reasonable probability of reconciliation before granting leave. Does not apply to nullity petitions.

chunks.title: Divorced Persons May Marry Again
chunks.short_summary: After a decree of divorce is made absolute, either party may remarry. A decree is absolute once no appeal is pending or appeal period expires.
chunks.detailed_notes: Remarriage before decree becoming absolute may constitute bigamy. No waiting period imposed by this Act after absolute decree.
scenario_tags: divorced person want to marry again

chunks.title: Legitimacy of Children of Void and Voidable Marriages
chunks.short_summary: Children of void or voidable marriages are deemed legitimate, even if the marriage is declared null and void by court. Such children have rights in the property of their parents only, not in joint family property of other relatives.
chunks.detailed_notes: This protects children from the consequences of their parents' invalid marriage. Does not extend inheritance rights to joint family or relatives.

chunks.title: Punishment for Bigamy
chunks.short_summary: Any marriage solemnized in contravention of Section 5(i) (bigamy) is punishable under Sections 494 and 495 of the Indian Penal Code (now BNS 2023). The second marriage is void.
chunks.detailed_notes: Section 494 IPC (BNS 82) penalizes marrying again during lifetime of existing spouse with up to 7 years and fine. Section 495 IPC (BNS 83) for concealment of former marriage.
scenario_tags: second marriage while first wife alive

chunks.title: Punishment for Contravention of Conditions for Marriage
chunks.short_summary: Violation of Section 5 conditions: (iii) underage marriage — simple imprisonment up to 15 days or fine up to Rs 1000 or both; (iv) prohibited degrees or (v) sapinda — simple imprisonment up to 1 month or fine up to Rs 1000 or both.
chunks.detailed_notes: Prosecution under PCMA 2006 is more common for child marriages as it provides more comprehensive protection.

chunks.title: Maintenance Pendente Lite and Expenses of Proceedings
chunks.short_summary: During pendency of any proceeding under HMA, court may order either spouse to pay the other a reasonable sum for maintenance (pendente lite) and litigation expenses, if that spouse has no independent income sufficient for support. This is interim maintenance during the case.
chunks.detailed_notes: Application should be disposed of within 60 days. Both husband and wife can apply. Amount determined by income, lifestyle, and expenses. Non-compliance is contempt of court.

chunks.title: Permanent Alimony and Maintenance
chunks.short_summary: Court may, at the time of decree or later, order either spouse to pay permanent alimony/maintenance to the other. Amount depends on income, property, and conduct of both parties, and standard of living during marriage. Can be modified if circumstances change.
chunks.detailed_notes: Permanent maintenance is not necessarily for life — it lapses if recipient remarries or lives in adultery. Either spouse (including husband) can claim. No fixed formula; judicial discretion guided by factors.

chunks.title: Custody, Maintenance and Education of Minor Children
chunks.short_summary: In any proceeding under HMA, the court may make orders as it deems just and proper regarding custody, maintenance, and education of minor children. The welfare of the child is the paramount consideration. Orders can be modified as circumstances change.
chunks.detailed_notes: Court considers child's age, sex, wishes (if old enough), parent's ability to care, any history of abuse or neglect. Interim custody can be granted during proceedings.

chunks.title: Disposal of Property
chunks.short_summary: In any proceeding under HMA, the court may make provisions as it deems just and proper for the settlement of property jointly owned by the parties.
chunks.detailed_notes: Covers matrimonial home and jointly acquired assets. Does not confer new property rights but allows equitable distribution of existing joint property.

chunks.title: Appeals from Decrees and Orders
chunks.short_summary: All decrees and orders made by the court in proceedings under the Hindu Marriage Act shall be appealable to the High Court within 30 days. Enforcement of decrees governed by Section 28-A.
chunks.detailed_notes: Section 28-A: Enforcement of decrees for restitution of conjugal rights and execution of orders.

chunks.title: Savings
chunks.short_summary: Any custom or usage governing marriage that was in force before the Act came into force, and any right, title, obligation or liability already acquired under prior laws, is saved to the extent not inconsistent with the Act.
chunks.detailed_notes: Preserves certain tribal customs, dissolution by custom in some communities, and pre-existing property rights.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Preliminary — Short Title, Application, Definitions, Overriding Effect
chunks.short_summary: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956 applies to Hindus (including Buddhists, Jains, Sikhs). Overrides inconsistent customs and laws. Defines 'maintenance', 'dependant', 'Hindu', 'sapinda'. Maintenance includes food, clothing, residence, education, and medical treatment.
chunks.detailed_notes: Act overrides prior Hindu law on adoption and maintenance to the extent inconsistent. Saved: customs expressly mentioned in the Act.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Requisites of a Valid Adoption
chunks.short_summary: For a valid adoption: (i) the person adopting must have the capacity to adopt; (ii) the person giving must have the capacity to give; (iii) the person being adopted must be capable of being adopted; (iv) the adoption must be made with due ceremony (giving and taking).
chunks.detailed_notes: All four elements must be present. Adoption is irrevocable once complete. Registered deed or evidence of giving-taking ceremony is important proof.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Capacity of a Male Hindu to Take in Adoption
chunks.short_summary: Any male Hindu of sound mind and not a minor may take a son or daughter in adoption. If married, he must obtain wife's consent (or each wife's consent if more than one). Adoption without wife's consent is void.
chunks.detailed_notes: Consent of wife can be dispensed with if she has renounced the world, has ceased to be Hindu, or been declared of unsound mind. A widower or unmarried male needs no consent.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Capacity of a Female Hindu to Take in Adoption
chunks.short_summary: An unmarried female Hindu, a widow, or a divorcée who is of sound mind and not a minor may take a son or daughter in adoption. A married woman cannot adopt independently — only her husband can adopt with her consent.
chunks.detailed_notes: After amendment to Section 7, married women can give consent but cannot independently adopt. Guardians and Wards Act may be used for adoption by married women de facto.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Persons Capable of Giving in Adoption
chunks.short_summary: Father or mother or guardian of a child can give the child in adoption. Father with consent of mother; if mother is dead or incapable, father alone. Guardian (court-appointed) can give with court permission. Unmarried mother can give without father's consent if father's identity unknown.
chunks.detailed_notes: Both biological parents' consent generally required for giving in adoption. Child given cannot be reclaimed after adoption is complete.
scenario_tags: who can give child for adoption

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Persons Who May Be Adopted
chunks.short_summary: A person to be adopted must be Hindu, not already adopted, below 15 years, unmarried — unless custom permits otherwise. No male child may be adopted if adopter already has a Hindu son, grandson or great-grandson (legitimate or adopted). Same rule for female child.
chunks.detailed_notes: Adoption of child over 15 or married permitted by custom in some communities. Adopted child severs ties with birth family and acquires rights in adoptive family.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Effects of Adoption
chunks.short_summary: An adopted child becomes the child of adoptive parents and ceases to be the child of birth parents for all purposes. The adopted child has the same rights and obligations in the adoptive family as a natural child.
chunks.detailed_notes: Adoption does not affect property already vested before adoption. Adopted child retains rights in adoptive family including succession. Cannot marry in prohibited degrees in either family.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Presumption as to Registered Documents Relating to Adoption
chunks.short_summary: A registered document purporting to record adoption and signed by the adoptive parent and the person giving the child shall be presumed correct, unless rebutted by clear evidence to the contrary.
chunks.detailed_notes: Registration provides evidentiary presumption. An unregistered adoption can still be proved by oral evidence of the giving-and-taking ceremony.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Prohibition of Certain Payments in Connection with Adoption
chunks.short_summary: No person shall give or receive any payment or reward in consideration of adoption. Violation is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months, fine, or both.
chunks.detailed_notes: Prevents trafficking or commercialization of adoption. Consistent with JJ Act provisions on inter-country adoption.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Maintenance of Wife
chunks.short_summary: A Hindu wife is entitled to be maintained by her husband during her lifetime. She is entitled to live separately and claim maintenance if husband is guilty of desertion, cruelty, has another wife living, keeps a concubine, has ceased to be Hindu, or has any other cause justifying her living apart. She cannot claim maintenance if she is unchaste or ceases to be Hindu.
chunks.detailed_notes: This is a civil remedy distinct from criminal remedy under CrPC 125. Amount determined by husband's income, property, and standard of living. Can file in family court or civil court.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Maintenance of Widowed Daughter-in-Law
chunks.short_summary: A widowed daughter-in-law whose husband is dead is entitled to maintenance from her father-in-law, provided she is unable to maintain herself from her own earnings or property and has no property from her husband or her parents' estate. Obligation ceases on her remarriage.
chunks.detailed_notes: Maintenance obligation on father-in-law is contingent and limited to his ability to pay from joint family property.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Maintenance of Children and Aged Parents
chunks.short_summary: A Hindu is bound to maintain his/her legitimate and illegitimate children (minor children unable to maintain themselves) and aged or infirm parents (including stepmother) who are unable to maintain themselves from their own earnings or property.
chunks.detailed_notes: Obligation is on the Hindu child regardless of joint or separate family. Includes unmarried daughters until marriage. Supreme Court has held that daughters are equally liable to maintain parents.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Dependants and Their Maintenance
chunks.short_summary: Section 21 defines 'dependants' as relatives of a deceased Hindu entitled to be maintained from his estate. Section 22 provides that heirs of a deceased Hindu are obliged to maintain his dependants out of the estate they inherit.
chunks.detailed_notes: Dependants include widow, minor sons, unmarried daughters, aged parents, widowed daughters, minor illegitimate children. Obligation limited to inherited estate.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956
chunks.title: Amount of Maintenance
chunks.short_summary: In determining the amount of maintenance, the court considers: position and status of parties; reasonable wants of claimant; whether claimant is living separately justifiably; income and property of both parties; number of other persons entitled to maintenance. Maintenance can be modified if circumstances change.
chunks.detailed_notes: No fixed formula. Section 25 provides that maintenance order is in rem and binds property. Section 24 governs priority of maintenance over other debts.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956
chunks.title: Preliminary — Short Title, Supplemental Nature, Application, Definitions
chunks.short_summary: The Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956 supplements the Guardians and Wards Act, 1890. Applies to Hindus. A 'minor' is a person under 18 years. Defines 'guardian', 'natural guardian', 'minor'.
chunks.detailed_notes: HMGA supplementary to GWA 1890. GWA applies to all communities; HMGA specifies rules for Hindus. Both courts (GWA court and civil court) have jurisdiction.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956
chunks.title: Natural Guardians of a Hindu Minor
chunks.short_summary: The natural guardian of a Hindu minor is: (a) for boy/unmarried girl — father, then mother; (b) for married minor girl — husband. Custody of child below 5 years ordinarily with mother. Natural guardian of illegitimate child is mother, then father.
chunks.detailed_notes: Father's right is primary but courts consider welfare paramount. Even father's natural guardianship can be overridden in child's interest. Stepfather/stepmother are not natural guardians.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956
chunks.title: Natural Guardianship of Adopted Son
chunks.short_summary: After adoption, the natural guardianship of the adopted son vests in the adoptive father and then adoptive mother, same as for natural children.
chunks.detailed_notes: Biological parents lose guardianship upon valid adoption.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956
chunks.title: Powers of Natural Guardian
chunks.short_summary: Natural guardian may do all acts necessary for benefit of minor's person and property. Court's permission required for: mortgaging or charging minor's property; transferring by sale, gift, exchange or otherwise any part of property; leasing beyond 5 years or beyond minor's majority. Violation makes act voidable at minor's option.
chunks.detailed_notes: Guardian cannot bind minor by a contract without court sanction for major transactions. Acts done for minor's benefit are binding. Court's permission is a safeguard.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956
chunks.title: Testamentary Guardians and Their Powers
chunks.short_summary: Father may appoint a guardian for minor by will. If mother is alive, her guardianship takes precedence unless she too appoints a testamentary guardian. The testamentary guardian has same powers as natural guardian.
chunks.detailed_notes: Mother can override father's testamentary guardian by acting as natural guardian. Testamentary guardian cannot override court-appointed guardian.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956
chunks.title: De Facto Guardian and Guardianship of Undivided Interest
chunks.short_summary: Section 11: A de facto guardian (person who acts as guardian without legal authority) cannot deal with minor's property. Section 12: No guardian shall be appointed for the undivided interest of a Hindu minor in joint family property — only the Karta manages it.
chunks.detailed_notes: De facto guardian's acts are unenforceable against the minor. Coparcenary interest governed by HUF law, not guardianship law.

chunks.act_name: The Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956
chunks.title: Welfare of Minor to Be Paramount Consideration
chunks.short_summary: In the appointment or declaration of any person as guardian of a Hindu minor, the court shall be guided solely by the welfare of the minor. In considering welfare, the court gives due weight to the minor's own wishes when old enough to express them.
chunks.detailed_notes: 'Welfare' includes physical, moral, emotional, educational and social wellbeing of the minor. Courts balance between parents' rights and child's best interests. Neither parent has an absolute right.

chunks.title: Preliminary — Short Title, Application, Definitions, Overriding Effect
chunks.short_summary: The Hindu Succession Act, 1956 governs intestate succession among Hindus. Applies to Hindus including Buddhists, Jains, Sikhs. Overrides prior laws and customs inconsistent with it. Defines 'agnate', 'cognate', 'full blood', 'half blood', 'heir', 'intestate'.
chunks.detailed_notes: Does not apply to property governed by Indian Succession Act 1925 or special custom. Testamentary succession also recognized under Section 30.

chunks.title: Devolution of Interest in Mitakshara Coparcenary Property
chunks.short_summary: By the Hindu Succession (Amendment) Act 2005, daughters are coparceners by birth in a Mitakshara coparcenary, with same rights and liabilities as sons. The daughter's interest devolves by testamentary or intestate succession upon her death. Applies irrespective of whether marriage was before or after amendment.
chunks.detailed_notes: Supreme Court in Vineeta Sharma v. Rakesh Sharma (2020) clarified daughters' coparcenary rights are by birth — retroactive and applies even if father died before 2005 amendment. Share equal to son's share.

chunks.title: Devolution of Interest in Tarwad, Tavazhi, Kutumba, Kavaru or Illom
chunks.short_summary: Governs devolution of interest in Marumakkattayam and Aliyasantana joint family systems (Kerala, Karnataka communities). Interest devolves on death to members of the family unit as provided for by this Act.
chunks.detailed_notes: Special provision for Nair and other matrilineal communities in Kerala and Karnataka.

chunks.title: General Rules of Succession for Males — Class I and Class II Heirs
chunks.short_summary: Property of a male Hindu dying intestate devolves as: (1) Class I heirs (simultaneously): son, daughter, widow, mother, son's son, son's daughter, son's widow, daughter's son, daughter's daughter, predeceased son's son/daughter/widow, predeceased daughter's son/daughter. (2) If no Class I heirs, then Class II heirs (sequentially). Class I heirs have priority. The Schedule lists all heirs.
chunks.detailed_notes: Among Class I heirs, all take simultaneously per stirpes. No preference of male over female in Class I. Widow and mother take equal shares with sons and daughters.
scenario_tags: who inherits property after husband dies
who gets property after father dies

chunks.title: Distribution Among Class I Heirs, Agnates, Cognates, and Computation of Degrees
chunks.short_summary: Section 10: Distribution of share among Class I heirs — widow's share, children's shares, mother's share. Section 11: Preferential treatment of agnates. Section 12: Order of succession among agnates and cognates. Section 13: Computation of degrees for agnate/cognate determination.
chunks.detailed_notes: Agnate = related through males only. Cognate = related through at least one female. Agnates preferred over cognates of same degree. Nearer degree preferred over more remote.

chunks.title: Property of a Female Hindu to Be Her Absolute Property
chunks.short_summary: Any property possessed by a female Hindu, whether acquired before or after the commencement of the Act, shall be held by her as full owner, not as a limited owner. Includes stridhan, inheritance, gift, purchase, partition, in lieu of maintenance or arrears. She has full power of disposal.
chunks.detailed_notes: Prior to this section, widows held limited estates (widow's estate). Now they have absolute ownership. Exception: property restricted by will or gift instrument creates limited right. Landmark change empowering Hindu women.

chunks.title: General Rules of Succession for Females
chunks.short_summary: Property of a female Hindu dying intestate devolves: (1) sons and daughters (including children of pre-deceased son/daughter) and husband; (2) heirs of husband; (3) father and mother; (4) heirs of father; (5) heirs of mother. Property inherited from parents reverts to father's heirs if no lineal descendants; property from husband reverts to husband's heirs.
chunks.detailed_notes: Unlike male succession, there is a reversion rule for property inherited from husband or parents. Section 16 gives the order of preference among the various categories.
scenario_tags: who gets wife's property after she dies

chunks.title: Preferential Rights, Child in Womb, Disqualifications, and Escheat
chunks.short_summary: Section 18: Full blood preferred over half blood heirs. Section 20: Child in womb at time of intestate's death has same right as a living child. Section 24: Widow remarrying loses right to inherit. Section 25: Murderer disqualified from inheriting. Section 29: Failure of heirs — property escheats to government.
chunks.detailed_notes: Converts and apostates not disqualified. Murderer disqualification established by common law and Section 25. Child in womb rule protects posthumous heirs.

chunks.title: Testamentary Succession
chunks.short_summary: A Hindu may dispose of by will or other testamentary disposition any property which is capable of being so disposed of, including his share in joint family property under Mitakshara coparcenary.
chunks.detailed_notes: Wills governed by Indian Succession Act 1925 for formalities. A Hindu can will his undivided share in HUF property now.

chunks.applicability: All citizens of India (civil marriage, inter-faith)
chunks.title: Short Title, Extent, Application and Definitions
chunks.short_summary: The Special Marriage Act, 1954 provides for civil marriage for all persons in India regardless of religion or faith. Applies throughout India. Defines 'customs', 'district court', 'marriage officer'.
chunks.detailed_notes: Enables inter-faith marriages without conversion. Persons who marry under SMA are governed by Indian Succession Act for property, not personal law.

chunks.title: Conditions for Solemnization of Special Marriages
chunks.short_summary: A marriage may be solemnized under this Act if: (a) neither party has a living spouse; (b) parties are of sound mind and capable of valid consent; (c) bridegroom is 21, bride is 18; (d) parties are not within prohibited degrees of relationship unless custom permits.
chunks.detailed_notes: Same conditions as Hindu Marriage Act but applies to all religions. Does not require any religious ceremony. Marriage officer solemnizes the marriage.

chunks.title: Notice, Objection, Registration and Solemnization of Special Marriage
chunks.short_summary: Parties must give written notice to marriage officer. 30 days notice period required (intended for objections). Any person may object to the marriage within 30 days. Marriage officer investigates and solemnizes if no valid objection. Marriage recorded in Marriage Certificate Book.
chunks.detailed_notes: 30-day notice period was challenged as invasion of privacy. In Safiya Sultana v. UP (2021), Allahabad HC struck down mandatory publication of notice as unconstitutional. Debate ongoing on privacy vs. public interest.

chunks.title: Restitution, Judicial Separation and Nullity Under SMA
chunks.short_summary: Section 22: Restitution of conjugal rights. Section 23: Judicial separation. Sections 24-26: Void and voidable marriages under SMA (same conditions as HMA). Court can declare marriage void or voidable for same grounds as HMA.
chunks.detailed_notes: Consistent with Hindu Marriage Act framework.

chunks.title: Divorce Under Special Marriage Act
chunks.short_summary: Either party to a Special Marriage may petition for divorce on grounds similar to those in HMA Section 13: adultery, desertion for 2 years, imprisonment, cruelty, venereal disease, leprosy, mental disorder, conversion, presumed death. Also: mutual consent divorce after one year separation.
chunks.detailed_notes: Ground of conversion not applicable under SMA as parties may be of different religion already. SMA divorce decree governed by court having jurisdiction.

chunks.title: Alimony and Custody Under SMA
chunks.short_summary: Section 36: Maintenance pendente lite (same as HMA Section 24). Section 37: Permanent alimony (same as HMA Section 25). Court may make orders for custody of children. Principles same as under Hindu Marriage Act.
chunks.detailed_notes: Provisions mirror HMA for maintenance and custody.

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Short Title and Definitions
chunks.short_summary: The Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005 protects women from domestic violence. Defines 'aggrieved person' (woman in domestic relationship), 'domestic relationship' (by blood, marriage, cohabitation), 'respondent' (male adult or relative), 'shared household', 'Protection Officer', 'Magistrate'.
chunks.detailed_notes: Live-in partners also covered as 'domestic relationship' includes women in relationships in nature of marriage. Extended to female relatives also in Hiral P. Harsora (2016).

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Definition of Domestic Violence
chunks.short_summary: Domestic violence means any act, omission or commission that: (a) harms, injures or endangers health, safety, life, limb or wellbeing (physical or mental); (b) causes harassment, harm, injury or danger to the woman. Includes physical abuse (assault, battery), sexual abuse, verbal/emotional abuse (insults, humiliation, name-calling, threat), and economic abuse (denial of financial resources, disposal of assets).
chunks.detailed_notes: Broad definition covers: hitting, slapping, kicking (physical); marital rape, forced sex (sexual); abusing, insulting about dowry, children, job (verbal/emotional); not giving money for daily needs, stopping from working (economic). Even a single act constitutes domestic violence.

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Duties of Police, Service Providers and Magistrate; Protection Officers
chunks.short_summary: Section 4: Police officer receiving domestic violence complaint must inform victim of rights and forward report. Section 5: Duties of police/service providers — inform about rights, Protection Officers, shelters, medical facilities. Section 8: Protection Officers (POs) assist victims. Section 9: POs prepare Domestic Incident Reports (DIR). Section 11: Duties of Government — maintain shelter homes, medical facilities.
chunks.detailed_notes: Victims can directly approach Magistrate under Section 12. POs are crucial — they prepare DIR, coordinate with court, ensure compliance. Victim should insist on DIR being prepared.
scenario_tags: who to call for domestic violence

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Application to Magistrate
chunks.short_summary: An aggrieved person, Protection Officer, or any other person on behalf of the aggrieved person may present an application to the Magistrate seeking various reliefs — protection order, residence order, monetary relief, custody order, compensation order.
chunks.detailed_notes: Application must be decided within 3 days. Notice to respondent within 3 days of application. Can seek multiple reliefs simultaneously. First hearing to be held within 3 days.
scenario_tags: how to file domestic violence case

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Right to Reside in Shared Household
chunks.short_summary: Every woman in a domestic relationship has the right to reside in the shared household, whether or not she has any right, title or beneficial interest in it. The respondent cannot dispossess the woman from the shared household or throw her out.
chunks.detailed_notes: Supreme Court in S.R. Batra v. Taruna Batra: shared household only includes where parties currently reside or have resided — does not extend to in-laws' entire property. But woman cannot be evicted without due process.

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Protection Orders
chunks.short_summary: Magistrate may pass a protection order prohibiting respondent from: committing further domestic violence acts; aiding or abetting the acts; entering the workplace or school of the woman or children; communicating with the woman; dispossessing the woman from the shared household; entering the shared household.
chunks.detailed_notes: Protection order is immediately executable. Breach of protection order is a criminal offence punishable under Section 31 (imprisonment up to 1 year or fine up to Rs 20,000 or both). Police must enforce it.

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Residence Orders
chunks.short_summary: Magistrate may pass a residence order: restraining respondent from dispossessing the woman; directing respondent to remove himself from shared household; restraining respondent or relatives from entering shared household; directing respondent to secure alternative accommodation or pay rent; directing respondent not to alienate or dispose of shared household.
chunks.detailed_notes: Woman is entitled to remain in shared household. If she left due to violence, court can order respondent to provide equivalent alternative. Order can include directing respondent to pay rent for her accommodation.
scenario_tags: right to stay in matrimonial home

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Monetary Reliefs
chunks.short_summary: Magistrate may direct respondent to pay monetary reliefs to the aggrieved person and children for: losses suffered due to domestic violence; medical expenses; loss of earnings; maintenance for woman and children (including women in live-in relationships). Relief can be additional to or in lieu of other maintenance orders.
chunks.detailed_notes: Monetary relief under PWDVA is broader than mere maintenance — covers medical, loss of earnings, property damage. Court can direct payment within specified time with default consequences.

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Custody Orders
chunks.short_summary: Magistrate may grant custody of children to the aggrieved person or other person on her behalf. Magistrate may also specify visiting rights of the respondent. Court acts in best interest of the child.
chunks.detailed_notes: Interim custody can be granted alongside protection order. Can be appealed and modified as circumstances change.
scenario_tags: child custody in domestic violence case

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Compensation Orders
chunks.short_summary: Magistrate may order the respondent to pay compensation to the aggrieved person for injuries, including mental torture and emotional distress, caused by acts of domestic violence.
chunks.detailed_notes: Compensation is separate from and in addition to monetary relief under Section 20. Covers mental and physical injury.

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Power to Grant Interim and Ex-Parte Orders
chunks.short_summary: If Magistrate is satisfied that domestic violence has occurred, an ex-parte interim order may be passed without notice to the respondent, on affidavit of the aggrieved person. This provides immediate protection.
chunks.detailed_notes: Ex-parte orders are urgent and immediate. Respondent can apply for modification on notice. Critical for immediate safety of the victim.

chunks.act_name: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
chunks.title: Procedure, Appeals, and Penalties for Breach
chunks.short_summary: Section 28: Proceedings under this Act governed by CrPC. Section 29: Appeals to Sessions Court within 30 days. Section 31: Breach of protection order is cognizable and non-bailable offence — punishment: imprisonment up to 1 year, fine up to Rs 20,000, or both. Subsequent breach: imprisonment of 2 years.
chunks.detailed_notes: Criminalization of breach makes protection orders enforceable. Police bound to register FIR on breach. Sessions Court appeal keeps review at higher level.

chunks.title: Nikah — Muslim Marriage
chunks.short_summary: Muslim marriage (Nikah) is a civil contract requiring: (1) offer (Ijab) by one party; (2) acceptance (Qubool) by other; (3) in presence of two male witnesses or one male and two female witnesses; (4) free consent of both parties. No religious ceremony mandatory but customary. Mahr (dower) is obligatory.
chunks.detailed_notes: Muslim law does not require registration. Sunni law: marriage valid with offer and acceptance in one meeting. Shia law: additional requirements. Muta marriage (temporary) is Shia-specific and not recognized in India as valid marriage.

chunks.title: Mahr — Muslim Dower
chunks.short_summary: Mahr is a sum of money or property that the husband is obliged to pay to the wife. It is an incident of marriage — not a gift. Prompt mahr is payable on demand; deferred mahr is payable on dissolution of marriage or death of husband. Wife can refuse conjugal rights until prompt mahr is paid.
chunks.detailed_notes: Mahr is debt on husband. Suit lies for recovery. Even after divorce, deferred mahr is payable. Minimum Hanafi mahr is 10 dirhams. Mahr must be specified; if not, proper mahr (mihr-i-mithl) applies.

chunks.title: Talaq — Muslim Divorce
chunks.short_summary: Forms of talaq: Talaq-ul-Sunnat (approved): (a) Ahsan — one revocable pronouncement during tuhr (purity period), followed by 90-day iddat; (b) Hasan — three pronouncements during three successive tuhrs. Talaq-ul-Biddat (disapproved triple talaq) — three simultaneous pronouncements — now criminalized by 2019 Act. Khula: wife-initiated dissolution with return of mahr. Mubarat: divorce by mutual consent.
chunks.detailed_notes: Iddat after talaq is 3 menstrual cycles or 3 months. Triple talaq abolished by Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act 2019. Women can seek dissolution under DMMA 1939.

chunks.title: Nafaqah — Muslim Maintenance
chunks.short_summary: Husband is obliged to maintain wife during marriage and during iddat period after divorce. After iddat, maintenance under Muslim personal law ends. Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act 1986 governs post-iddat maintenance — provides fair and reasonable provision but limited. Shah Bano case (1985) led to legislative intervention via 1986 Act.
chunks.detailed_notes: Post Shah Bano controversy: Danial Latifi v. Union of India (2001) — Supreme Court held 1986 Act must be interpreted to provide fair provision for entire life. CrPC 125 can still be invoked by Muslim women.
scenario_tags: Muslim woman financial rights after talaq

chunks.title: Muslim Personal Law (Shariat) Application Act, 1937
chunks.short_summary: Declares that Muslim personal law (Shariat) shall apply to Muslims in matters of marriage, dissolution of marriage, mahr, dower, maintenance, guardianship, gifts, trusts, waqfs, and succession. Overrides any conflicting custom or usage.
chunks.detailed_notes: Pre-codified personal law declaration. Ensures Shariat governs unless specific codified statute applies (like DMMA 1939 or MWPRD 2019).

chunks.title: Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939 — Grounds for Dissolution
chunks.short_summary: A Muslim woman may seek dissolution of marriage on grounds: husband missing for 4 years; husband failed to maintain for 2 years; husband sentenced to 7+ years imprisonment; husband impotent since marriage; husband insane for 2+ years or has leprosy or venereal disease; wife married below 15 (can repudiate before 18); husband treats wife cruelly (physical cruelty, false accusations, disposing of wife's property, obstructing religious practice, maintaining other wives unequally, associating with disreputable women).
chunks.detailed_notes: Only the wife can seek dissolution under this Act. She must file petition in civil court. DMMA combined khula (court-decreed dissolution) for Muslim women. Court decree makes it Faskh.

chunks.act_name: Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986
chunks.title: Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986
chunks.short_summary: After divorce, a Muslim woman is entitled to: (1) Reasonable and fair provision and maintenance within the iddat period by ex-husband; (2) Mahr or dower; (3) Return of property given to her by relatives or husband before, during or after marriage. If she cannot maintain herself after iddat, Waqf Board or her relatives (liable to maintain under Muslim law) must pay. She can also approach Magistrate for order.
chunks.detailed_notes: Danial Latifi case (2001): SC held 'provision' means for post-divorce future needs too — not limited to iddat period. Gives Muslim women substantive rights despite being outside Shariat's maintenance obligation after divorce.

chunks.act_name: Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act, 2019
chunks.title: Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act, 2019 — Criminalization of Triple Talaq
chunks.short_summary: Declares triple talaq (talaq-i-biddat — three simultaneous pronouncements) void, illegal, and unconstitutional. Husband pronouncing triple talaq liable to imprisonment up to 3 years and fine. Wife can seek subsistence allowance and custody of minor children from Magistrate. Act applies despite any custom or practice.
chunks.detailed_notes: SC in Shayara Bano (2017) declared triple talaq unconstitutional. This Act gave it criminal teeth. Complaint can be made only by the aggrieved wife or her blood relatives. Cognizable (for purpose of arrest) but non-bailable at Magistrate discretion.
scenario_tags: Muslim woman protection after triple talaq

chunks.title: Muslim Law of Inheritance — Sunni and Shia
chunks.short_summary: Muslim inheritance is governed by Shariat. Sunni Hanafi law: three classes of heirs — Sharers (Quranic heirs), Residuaries (Asabat), Distant kindred. Shia law: two classes — Quranic heirs and blood relations. No concept of joint family. Each individual owns separately and property devolves as per Shariat on death.
chunks.detailed_notes: Widow's share: 1/8 if there are children, 1/4 if no children. Daughter's share: half of son's share. No coparcenary concept in Muslim law. Wills restricted to 1/3 of estate without heirs' consent.

chunks.title: Muslim Law of Guardianship
chunks.short_summary: Under Muslim law, father is the legal guardian of minor's property; mother has right to custody (hizanat) of young children. Hizanat: mother entitled to custody of son till age 7 (Hanafi) and daughter till puberty. After these ages, father takes over. Best interest of child considered.
chunks.detailed_notes: Courts apply GWA 1890 for appointment of guardian. Welfare of minor is paramount even under Muslim law per judiciary.

chunks.title: Christian Marriage — Indian Christian Marriage Act, 1872
chunks.short_summary: Marriage among Christians governed by Indian Christian Marriage Act, 1872. Marriage solemnized by Minister of Religion, Marriage Registrar, or in presence of Marriage Registrar. Notice period of 21 days required. One party must be Christian. Registration mandatory. Bigamy prohibited.
chunks.detailed_notes: Indian Divorce Act 1869 governs divorce for Christians. Special Marriage Act 1954 can also be used for civil marriage.

chunks.title: Divorce Under Indian Divorce Act, 1869
chunks.short_summary: Christians may seek divorce under Indian Divorce Act 1869 (amended 2001). Grounds: adultery; conversion from Christianity; incurable unsoundness of mind (2 years); leprosy (2 years); venereal disease (2 years); desertion (2 years); cruelty. Mutual consent divorce after 2 years separation. District Court has jurisdiction.
chunks.detailed_notes: Prior to 2001 amendment, husband could divorce only for adultery while wife needed adultery plus another ground — the discriminatory provision was struck down. Now equal grounds.

chunks.title: Alimony and Maintenance Under Indian Divorce Act
chunks.short_summary: Court may grant alimony pendente lite (interim) and permanent alimony to either spouse. Amount based on parties' income, conduct, and circumstances. Section 37 of Indian Divorce Act — permanent alimony is discretionary.
chunks.detailed_notes: Provisions broadly similar to HMA. CrPC 125 also available as additional remedy.

chunks.applicability: Christians, Parsis, and persons married under SMA
chunks.title: Indian Succession Act, 1925 — Intestate and Testamentary Succession
chunks.short_summary: Governs succession for Christians and Parsis (intestate and testamentary). Also governs wills and probate for all communities except Muslims and Hindus (for intestate succession). Key: Part V (intestate succession for Christians): widow gets 1/3, children get 2/3; if no children, widow gets 1/2. Wills: testator must be 18+, of sound mind, sign in presence of two witnesses.
chunks.detailed_notes: Also applies to persons married under Special Marriage Act 1954 for succession purposes. Probate jurisdiction exercised by High Courts and District Courts.

chunks.act_name: The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936
chunks.title: Parsi Marriage — The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936
chunks.short_summary: Marriage among Parsis solemnized by Parsi priest (Dastur or Mobed) in presence of two Parsi witnesses. Both parties must be Parsi. Ashirvad ceremony required. Marriage Registrar also present. Bigamy prohibited. Grounds for divorce: adultery, cruelty, desertion (2 years), insanity, conversion, venereal disease, leprosy.
chunks.detailed_notes: Matrimonial Court for Parsis established with assessors from Parsi community. Divorce proceedings have unique Parsi community involvement.

chunks.title: Power of Court to Make Order as to Guardianship
chunks.short_summary: Court may appoint a guardian of the person or property (or both) of a minor if it is satisfied that such appointment is for the welfare of the minor. Any person interested may apply. Court considers religion, character, capacity of guardian, preference of minor (if old enough), wishes of deceased parents.
chunks.detailed_notes: General secular law applicable to all communities. Hindu minors governed additionally by HMGA 1956. Muslim minors governed additionally by Muslim personal law on guardianship.

chunks.title: Matters to be Considered by Court
chunks.short_summary: In appointing or declaring a guardian, the court considers the welfare of the minor as paramount. Also considers: age, sex, religion of minor; character and capacity of proposed guardian; preference of minor (if old enough); wishes of deceased parents; existing relationships.
chunks.detailed_notes: Welfare is the supreme consideration — even outweighs natural guardianship rights if welfare demands.

chunks.title: Family Courts Act, 1984 — Establishment and Jurisdiction
chunks.short_summary: States with population over 1 million required to establish Family Courts for speedy disposal of family disputes. Family Court has jurisdiction over: matrimonial disputes (divorce, separation, nullity, restitution); maintenance; guardianship and custody; property disputes between spouses; declarations of legitimacy and marriage. Conciliation and counselling emphasized before adversarial proceedings.
chunks.detailed_notes: Lawyers have no right of audience as of right — parties appear in person or through social welfare organizations. Strict evidence rules relaxed. Court-appointed counsellors aid settlement.

chunks.act_name: Code of Criminal Procedure, Section 125 (now BNSS Section 144)
chunks.applicability: All persons in India irrespective of religion
chunks.title: Order for Maintenance of Wives, Children and Parents (CrPC Sec 125 / BNSS Sec 144)
chunks.short_summary: Magistrate may order a person with sufficient means who neglects or refuses to maintain: (a) his wife unable to maintain herself; (b) legitimate or illegitimate minor child unable to maintain itself; (c) legitimate or illegitimate child (not minor) with physical or mental abnormality; (d) father or mother unable to maintain themselves — to pay monthly maintenance. Applies to all religions. Quick remedy in criminal court.
chunks.detailed_notes: Faster than civil court remedy. Available to all women including Muslim, Hindu, Christian, etc. No ceiling on amount now. Interim maintenance also available under Section 125(3). Now under BNSS 2023 as Section 144 but substantively same. Wife includes divorced wife who has not remarried, during proceedings.

chunks.title: Prohibition of Child Marriage Act, 2006 — Child Marriage Prohibition
chunks.short_summary: Prohibits child marriage (boy under 21, girl under 18). A contracting child can seek annulment within 2 years of attaining majority. Voidable (not void) at option of the minor party. Child marriages may be declared void in certain circumstances (force, trafficking). Persons marrying minor, parents/guardians allowing such marriage, and solemnizers are punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine up to Rs 1 lakh.
chunks.detailed_notes: Child Marriage Prohibition Officers appointed in each district. Injunctions can be sought to prevent imminent child marriage. Court of JMFC has jurisdiction. Pending proposal to make all child marriages void.

chunks.act_name: Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961 and IPC Section 498-A (BNS Section 85)
chunks.title: Dowry Prohibition and Cruelty to Married Woman
chunks.short_summary: Dowry Prohibition Act 1961: giving or taking dowry is punishable with imprisonment of 5 years and fine. Dowry means property demanded as condition of marriage. IPC Section 498-A (BNS Section 85): Husband or husband's relative subjecting married woman to cruelty (likely to cause death or injury or to coerce her to meet unlawful demands including dowry) is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years. Section 304-B IPC (BNS 80): Dowry death — death within 7 years of marriage under suspicious circumstances — punishable with 7 years to life.

chunks.title: Waqf Act, 1995 — Islamic Charitable Endowment
chunks.short_summary: A Waqf is dedication of property by a Muslim for religious, pious or charitable purposes. Governed by Waqf Act 1995. Central Waqf Council and State Waqf Boards regulate waqfs. Mutawalli manages the waqf property. Waqf Tribunal adjudicates disputes.
chunks.detailed_notes: Waqf analogous to Hindu religious endowment. Once created, waqf is irrevocable. Family waqf (waqf-alal-aulad) for descendants is also valid.

chunks.act_name: Andhra Pradesh Compulsory Registration of Marriages Act, 2002
chunks.applicability: All persons in Andhra Pradesh and Telangana
chunks.title: Compulsory Registration of Marriages in AP/Telangana
chunks.short_summary: All marriages (regardless of religion) in AP and Telangana must be registered within 30 days of solemnization. Non-registration is an offence. Certificate of registration is evidentiary proof. Revenue Divisional Officers are Marriage Registrars.
chunks.detailed_notes: Part of broader push for compulsory marriage registration. Similar acts in other states. Provides women legal protection through documented marriages.

chunks.applicability: Hindus in Andhra Pradesh and Telangana
chunks.title: AP Amendment — Daughters as Coparceners (Pre-2005)
chunks.short_summary: AP was a pioneer state that amended Hindu Succession Act in 1986 to give daughters equal coparcenary rights in Mitakshara joint family property, nearly 19 years before the national 2005 amendment. Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra also made similar amendments. The 2005 national amendment superseded these.
chunks.detailed_notes: Historic reform. Set precedent for national amendment. Daughters in AP/Telangana have had coparcenary rights since 1986.

chunks.act_name: Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015
chunks.title: Juvenile Justice Act, 2015 — Adoption and Child Care
chunks.short_summary: The JJ Act 2015 provides for adoption of children through CARA (Central Adoption Resource Authority). Governs inter-country adoption, foster care, and child care institutions. All adoptions of abandoned, surrendered, or orphaned children must go through CARA-registered agencies. Prospective adoptive parents from any religion can adopt under JJ Act.
chunks.detailed_notes: JJ Act adoption is secular and applies to all religions. For Hindu families, adoption under HAMA 1956 (gives full Hindu adoption effect) is additional option. JJ Act adoption: child severs ties with birth family.
